summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the definition of 'Mean (x̄)'?

"Mean (x̄)" means: The arithmetic average of all data values, calculated by summing all values and dividing by n; sensitive to outliers and skewness. The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Median'?

"Median" means: The middle value when data are ordered from least to greatest; resistant to outliers and preferred for skewed distributions. The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Interquartile range (IQR)'?

"Interquartile range (IQR)" means: The difference between Q3 (75th percentile) and Q1 (25th percentile), measuring the spread of the middle 50% of data. The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Five-number summary'?

"Five-number summary" means: Minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum—the basis for constructing boxplots and identifying outliers. The other options describe different concepts.

What is the definition of 'Outlier (1.5×IQR rule)'?

"Outlier (1.5×IQR rule)" means: A data point falling more than 1.5 × IQR below Q1 or above Q3, potentially indicating unusual observations or data errors. The other options describe different concepts.

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

"Outlier (1.5×IQR rule)" is defined as: A data point falling more than 1.5 × IQR below Q1 or above Q3, potentially indicating unusual observations or data errors. The other options describe different concepts.

This description refers to "Mean (x̄)".

This description refers to "Median".

This description refers to "Five-number summary".
